questions.question: Define stack and queue data structures. Explain the principles they follow with real-world examples. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Stack: Linear data structure following LIFO principle. Example: Stack of plates.
Queue: Linear data structure following FIFO principle. Example: People in a line.
Stack operations: push, pop, peek. Queue operations: enqueue, dequeue, front.
Both are linear data structures but differ in insertion/removal policies.

questions.question: Explain overflow and underflow conditions in stacks and queues with examples. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Overflow: Trying to add element when structure is full (push to full stack, enqueue to full queue).
Underflow: Trying to remove element when structure is empty (pop from empty stack, dequeue from empty queue).
Before push/enqueue: check isFull. Before pop/dequeue: check isEmpty.
In array implementation, overflow happens when top == MAX-1 (stack) or rear == MAX-1 (queue).

questions.question: Compare and contrast stacks and queues. Discuss their applications with examples. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Stack: LIFO, single-end operations (top). Queue: FIFO, two-end operations (front, rear).
Stack applications: Function calls (call stack), expression evaluation (postfix), undo/redo, backtracking.
Queue applications: CPU scheduling, printer spooling, BFS traversal, buffering.
Both can be implemented using arrays or linked lists.
Time complexity: O(1) for basic operations in both.

questions.question: Write the algorithms for push and pop operations on a stack implemented using an array. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Push: Check if stack is full (top == MAX-1), if not increment top and add element at stack[top].
Pop: Check if stack is empty (top == -1), if not return stack[top] and decrement top.
